title: Newborn Breastfeeding Guide
content:
Breastfeeding should start as soon as possible after birth, ideally within 1 hour of delivery.

## Feeding Frequency
- Newborns need 8-12 feedings per day
- Feed on demand, no need for strict schedules
- Each feeding session lasts approximately 15-20 minutes

## Signs Your Baby Is Getting Enough
- 6 or more wet diapers per day
- Stools transition from black meconium to yellow
- Baby appears satisfied after feeding
- Steady weight gain

## Common Issues
- Sore nipples: Adjust latch position
- Insufficient milk supply: Increase feeding frequency and ensure adequate rest

title: Formula Feeding Essentials
content:
If breastfeeding is not possible, infant formula is a safe alternative.

## Preparation Method
- Add water first, then formula powder
- Follow the ratio on the package instructions, don't improvise with concentration
- Use water at 40-50°C temperature
- Gently shake to mix, avoiding excessive bubbles

## Feeding Amount Reference
- Newborn: 60-90ml per feeding
- 1-2 months: 90-120ml per feeding
- 3-4 months: 120-180ml per feeding
- 5-6 months: 180-240ml per feeding

## Important Notes
- Finish prepared formula within 2 hours at room temperature
- Do not reheat leftover formula
- Thoroughly wash and sterilize bottles after each use

title: Newborn Jaundice Monitoring
content:
Most newborns develop physiological jaundice, typically appearing 2-3 days after birth.

## Physiological Jaundice
- Appears 2-3 days after birth
- Peaks at 4-5 days
- Resolves naturally by 7-14 days
- Baby appears alert and feeds normally

## When to Seek Medical Attention
- Jaundice appears within 24 hours of birth
- Jaundice persists beyond 2 weeks
- Yellowing extends to palms and soles
- Accompanied by fever, poor feeding, or excessive sleepiness
- Pale or grayish stools

title: Solid Food Introduction Guide
content:
The World Health Organization recommends starting solids when your baby reaches 6 months old.

## Principles for Introducing Solids
- Start with single-ingredient foods
- Progress from thin to thick, fine to coarse textures
- Introduce only one new food at a time
- Observe for 3-5 days before introducing another food to watch for allergies

## Recommended for 6-8 Months
- Iron-fortified rice cereal (preferred first food)
- Vegetable purees: pumpkin, potato, carrot
- Fruit purees: apple, banana, pear
- Small amounts of egg yolk (start with 1/4)

## Recommended for 8-10 Months
- Minced meat and fish
- Soft pasta and porridge
- Finger foods: steamed vegetable strips

## Important Reminders
- No salt, sugar, or honey before age 1
- Avoid choking hazards like whole nuts
- Solids do not replace milk; breast milk or formula remains the primary nutrition source

title: 6-12 Month Developmental Milestones
content:
Every baby develops at their own pace; the following are general references.

## Gross Motor Skills
- 6 months: Can sit with support
- 7-8 months: Sits independently
- 8-9 months: Begins crawling
- 10-12 months: Pulls to stand, cruises along furniture

## Fine Motor Skills
- 6-7 months: Grasps with both hands
- 8-9 months: Pincer grasp with thumb and forefinger
- 10-12 months: Drinks from a cup

## Language
- 6-8 months: Produces syllables like ba-ba, ma-ma
- 9-12 months: Understands simple commands (like "wave bye-bye")

## When to Be Concerned
- Cannot sit by 9 months
- No vocalizations by 12 months
- No response to sounds
If you have concerns, consult your pediatrician

title: 1-2 Year Diet Transition
content:
After age 1, babies gradually transition from milk-based to food-based nutrition.

## Meal Structure
- 3 main meals + 2 snacks per day
- Maintain 400-500ml of milk daily
- Foods progressively resemble adult diet (but softer and finer)

## Nutrition Highlights
- Ensure adequate iron intake (red meat, liver)
- One egg per day
- Adequate vegetables and fruits
- Variety of grains (rice, noodles, steamed buns)

## Important Notes
- Small amounts of salt can be added (not exceeding 1g per day)
- Avoid high-sugar snacks and sugary beverages
- Encourage self-feeding
- Eat together as a family to create a positive eating environment
